EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved northward across southern New England.  This brought a period of mainly rain and warm temperatures.  In addition, a strong low level jet (up to 80 kts) resulted in high winds across much of southern New England.  There was some tree damage and downed power lines with winds gusting to 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator recorded a wind gust to 52 mph on their home weather station in Coventry.  A tree was downed blocking a road in West Greenwich.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved northward across southern New England.  This brought a period of mainly rain and warm temperatures.  In addition, a strong low level jet (up to 80 kts) resulted in high winds across much of southern New England.  There was some tree damage and downed power lines with winds gusting to 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at T.F. Green State Airport in Warwick (KPVD) recorded a wind gust to 60 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved northward across southern New England.  This brought a period of mainly rain and warm temperatures.  In addition, a strong low level jet (up to 80 kts) resulted in high winds across much of southern New England.  There was some tree damage and downed power lines with winds gusting to 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Weatherflow reporting site at Bristol recorded a wind gust of 63 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved northward across southern New England.  This brought a period of mainly rain and warm temperatures.  In addition, a strong low level jet (up to 80 kts) resulted in high winds across much of southern New England.  There was some tree damage and downed power lines with winds gusting to 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operators in Westerly and South Kingstown reported wind gusts to 56 and 55 mph, respectively, on their home weather stations.  In Richmond, a tree was downed near Richmond Elementary School on Kingston Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Andrea tracked across southeastern Massachusetts bringing heavy rain (3-5 inches) to much of southern New England.  This resulted in significant urban flooding, particularly across eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island, as well as river and small stream flooding.  It also contributed to record high rainfall across the area for the month of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of rain fell across the Providence metro area.  Several basements were flooded along Lockbridge Street in Pawtucket.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved out of the midwest, off the mid-Atlantic coast and northeastward across Nantucket and the outer arm of Cape Cod bringing accumulating snow to much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to eight inches of snow fell across northwestern Providence County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved out of the midwest, off the mid-Atlantic coast and northeastward across Nantucket and the outer arm of Cape Cod bringing accumulating snow to much of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to six inches of snow fell across western Kent County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Southern New England, resulting in strong cold air advection and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and wires were downed on Howard Avenue in Scituate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved northward across southern New England.  This brought a period of mainly rain and warm temperatures.  In addition, a strong low level jet (up to 80 kts) resulted in high winds across much of southern New England.  There was some tree damage and downed power lines with winds gusting to 60 to 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported wind gusts up to 64 mph in Scituate.  The media reported gusts to 51 mph in Woonsocket.  A primary power line was downed on East School Street in Woonsocket.  In Glocester, a tree was downed across Snake Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary warm front draped across southern New England, coupled with a very moist atmosphere, resulted in showers and thunderstorms across the area for the third day in a row.  Heavy rain fell within these showers and storms and flash flooding occurred, particularly over portions of Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency room of Memorial Hospital flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through an unstable atmosphere across southern New England, triggering showers and thunderstorms across much of Massachusetts and Rhode Island. There was enough shear and instability for some of these storms to become severe, producing damaging winds.  In addition, because of the very moist atmosphere and heavy rain over the previous two days, flash flooding also occurred in several locations.

There was enough shear and instability for some of these storms to become severe, producing damaging winds.  In addition, because of the very moist atmosphere and heavy rain over the previous two days, flash flooding also occurred in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large branch and wires on Plymouth Drive were downed by thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through an unstable atmosphere across southern New England, triggering showers and thunderstorms across much of Massachusetts and Rhode Island. There was enough shear and instability for some of these storms to become severe, producing damaging winds.  In addition, because of the very moist atmosphere and heavy rain over the previous two days, flash flooding also occurred in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Main Street was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved over southern New England bringing showers and thunderstorms to the region. Precipitable water values over two inches indicated a very moist atmosphere; in addition, weak winds at the mid levels resulted in very slow moving storms. Both of these things led to torrential rainfall and flash flooding.  This is the second day in a row that parts of Kent County received between three and four inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell across Coventry, RI resulting in substantial flash flooding.  Route 117 was flooded at several points, including near Route 116 under the railroad trestle, near South Main, and near Paine Memorial Field.  Many cars became stuck in flood waters along Route 117.  The area near 1070 Tiogue Avenue was flooded and impassable.  The intersection of Hopkins Hill Road and Tiogue Avenue was flooded with four cars stuck in the flood waters.  The intersection of Laurel Avenue and Washington Street was flooded and impassable.  Hopkins Hill Road was closed due to flooding between South Main Street and Route 3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary warm front draped across southern New England, coupled with a very moist atmosphere, resulted in showers and thunderstorms across the area for the third day in a row.  Heavy rain fell within these showers and storms and flash flooding occurred, particularly over portions of Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Plainfield Pike was flooded with water about six inches deep at the ramp to Interstate 295.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical moisture in place across southern New England ahead of a cold front resulted in precipitable water values increasing to 2-2.5 inches (2 to 3 times normal).  The cold front provided enough lift for widespread rain with embedded thunderstorms across all of southern New England.  The high moisture content of the atmosphere resulted in torrential downpours across much of the region, which led to flash flooding in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 114 in Warren was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved over southern New England bringing showers and thunderstorms to the region. Precipitable water values over two inches indicated a very moist atmosphere; in addition, weak winds at the mid levels resulted in very slow moving storms. Both of these things led to torrential rainfall and flash flooding.  This is the second day in a row that parts of Kent County received between three and four inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Providence Journal reported that two horses died in a barn fire in Hopkinton caused by a lightning strike.  The horses were 6 and 17 years old.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough moved into southern New England, providing lift for showers and thunderstorms to develop in a hot and humid atmosphere.  In addition, CAPE up to 2000 J/kg and 30-40 kts of shear provided enough instability and shear for thunderstorms to become severe.  Because of warm air aloft, these thunderstorms mostly produced strong to damaging winds and not large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 90 year old woman died at the Charlesgate Nursing Center in Providence.  The woman was not in poor health but lived in a room without air conditioning.  Despite air conditioned rooms being available to the woman, she remained in her room. The Rhode Island Medical Examiner's Office confirmed that the cause of death was hyperthermia or extreme heat. A heat advisory was in effect for southeast Providence County, with heat index values up to 102 expected.  Heat index values reached 92 in Providence by 8am, peaked at 101 at 2pm and remained there for 3 hours before temperatures fell below 80 after a thunderstorm.  Time of death is approximate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong pressure rises behind a cold front coupled with cold air advection resulted in strong to damaging winds across much of southern New England. Most of the damage was to trees, some of which were still at least partially leaved.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at Westerly State Airport (KWST) recorded a wind gust of 46 mph.  In Charlestown, a tree was downed on Kings Factory Road near Indian Cedar Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong low level jet coupled with strong pressure falls associated with a low pressure region approaching southern New England resulted in strong to damaging winds across southern New England. Damage was largely to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Surface Observing System at T.F. Green State Airport in Warwick (KPVD) recorded sustained wind speeds of 36 mph and wind gusts of 49 mph.  In addition, a tree was downed onto a house on Cedar Drive in West Warwick, resulting in a house fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong low level jet coupled with strong pressure falls associated with a low pressure region approaching southern New England resulted in strong to damaging winds across southern New England. Damage was largely to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator recorded a wind gust of 59 mph on their home weather station in South Kingstown.  A utility pole was downed onto Route 102 in Exeter. A tree and wires were downed in Kingston, temporarily disrupting Amtrak train service.  A large tree was downed onto a house in North Kingstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong low level jet coupled with strong pressure falls associated with a low pressure region approaching southern New England resulted in strong to damaging winds across southern New England. Damage was largely to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was downed in Bristol.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trough moved into southern New England, providing lift for showers and thunderstorms to develop in a hot and humid atmosphere. Because of warm air aloft, these thunderstorms mostly produced strong to damaging winds and not large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 146 was flooded at Twin River Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm moved across the southern New England coastline, spreading a wintry mix of precipitation across much of the area.  While the precipitation started as snow for most locations, snow quickly transitioned to sleet and freezing rain across portions of Connecticut, Rhode Island, and southeastern Massachusetts.  Areas of northern Massachusetts and southwestern New Hampshire remained all snow and received around a foot of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to five inches of snow fell across western Kent County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure off the coast of southern New England coupled with a shortwave/mid level trough in the Ohio Valley resulted in a typical Norlun trough set up across the area.  These troughs can promote a very narrow band of heavy snow where low level convergence sets up.  This can result in areas in the band having much more snow than areas outside of the band.  This set up spread snow across much of the area with the highest amounts occurring along the south coast of Massachusetts and into Newport County, Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of snow fell across Newport County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This storm brought heavy snow and significant coastal flooding to the forecast area. This was an unusual synoptic set-up, with low pressure lingering off the coast of southern New England for several days. Snowfall was difficult to forecast due to concerns about precipitation type and boundary layer temperature. In the end, precipitation type turned out to be all snow for much of the area, with most locations receiving 1 to 2 feet of snow.  In addition, the Massachusetts east coast was hit by widespread moderate and pockets of major coastal flooding for two high tide cycles and beach erosion for at least 5 high tide cycles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to seven inches of snow fell across Bristol County.

Also, several basements were flooded along Brook Street.  In Cranston, Park and Reservoir Avenues were flooded and impassable.

It also contributed to record high rainfall across the area for the month of June.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to six inches of rain fell across Washington County.  In Charlestown, Route 1, Route 112, Old Coach Road, and Klondike Road all were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary warm front draped across southern New England, coupled with a very moist atmosphere, resulted in showers and thunderstorms across the area for the third day in a row.  Heavy rain fell within these showers and storms and flash flooding occurred, particularly over portions of Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the same part of Rhode Island for the second day in a row resulted in widespread flash flooding across much of Kent County and Cranston.  Information on flooding in Kent County can be found in a separate event entry for Kent County.  In Cranston, a car became stuck in flood waters in a rotary, stranding a man on the roof of the car.  A car flooded at the intersection of Wellington and Essex Streets resulted in a water rescue done by boat.  The fire department rescued occupants of a car stuck in flood waters on Oaklawn Avenue.  An area of Cranston Street including the 1500 block was flooded with one to two feet of water.  Dean Estates apartment buildings were evacuated by boat by the fire department after flood waters inundated the basement/garden level apartments up to the ceilings.  When firefighters arrived at the apartments, water was already four feet deep.  One firefighter was injured when the first floor collapsed because of the basement flooding.  About 60 people were evacuated from the complex.  One lane of Budlong Road was damaged by flooding.  Reservoir and Pontiac Avenues were flooded and impassable.  Route 10 south at Interstate 95 was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through an unstable atmosphere across southern New England, triggering showers and thunderstorms across much of Massachusetts and Rhode Island. There was enough shear and instability for some of these storms to become severe, producing damaging winds.  In addition, because of the very moist atmosphere and heavy rain over the previous two days, flash flooding also occurred in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees were downed by thunderstorm winds, including one on Devonshire Drive, another on Opechee Drive, and a couple on the golf course at Rhode Island Country Club.  Large branches also were downed on Middle Highway, Nayatt Road, Plymouth Drive and at Primrose Hill Cemetery near Barrington Town Hall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through an unstable atmosphere across southern New England, triggering showers and thunderstorms across much of Massachusetts and Rhode Island. There was enough shear and instability for some of these storms to become severe, producing damaging winds.  In addition, because of the very moist atmosphere and heavy rain over the previous two days, flash flooding also occurred in several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were downed along Market Street by thunderstorm winds determined to be associated with a microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary warm front draped across southern New England, coupled with a very moist atmosphere, resulted in showers and thunderstorms across the area for the third day in a row.  Heavy rain fell within these showers and storms and flash flooding occurred, particularly over portions of Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the same part of Rhode Island for the second day in a row resulted in widespread flash flooding across much of Kent County and Cranston.  Information on flooding in Cranston can be found in a separate event entry for Providence County.  In Coventry this included, Route 102 flooded with up to a foot of water, numerous roads flooded with one to two feet of water, including Route 117, Hopkins Hill and Sandy Bottom Roads, and Laurel and Tiogue Avenues, with cars stuck in flood waters on all of those roads.  Hopkins Hill Road, Sandy Bottom Road, and Route 33 were later closed.  Route 116 (Knotty Oak Road) was washed out at Gervais Street.  In West Greenwich, Route 102 was closed at the Interstate 95 interchange with several feet of water flooding the road.  A couple was rescued from a stranded car on Route 3 near the high school.  In Warwick, three feet of water flowed over Bald Hill Road.  Post Road and Airport Road near T.F. Green Airport were flooded and impassable.  Parts of Post Road were closed.  Hardig Brook overflowed its banks, flooding Cedar Pond Drive at the Royal Crest Estates.  In West Warwick, a retaining wall collapsed at the intersection of New London Avenue and Providence Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very warm, moist airmass remained in place across Southern New England with precipitable water values over 2 inches.  Showers and thunderstorms that developed with an upper level disturbance ahead of a cold front resulted in very heavy rainfall with reports of near 2 inches in 30 minutes in some locations.  This resulted in flooding particularly in southeastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Atwood Avenue and Pontiac Avenue in Cranston were flooded.  A car was stuck in the flood waters on Pontiac Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An historic winter storm deposited tremendous amounts of snow over all of southern New England, mainly from the mid-afternoon on Friday, February 8 and lasting into the daylight hours of Saturday, February 9. What made this an amazing storm was the widespread coverage of heavy snowfall.  Most locations received 2 to 2.5 feet of snow!  Isolated thunderstorms were common across the entire region during the height of the storm.||A low pressure system advancing from the Great Lakes region combined forces with a very moist low pressure system moving northeast from the Gulf Coast states. Explosive deepening took place Friday evening, February 8, as a low center moved from the North Carolina coast to south of Nantucket.  Strong high pressure to the north of New England helped ensure that cold air remained in place over the area. Snowfall gained intensity during the afternoon, but during the night, 2 to 3 inch per hour amounts were common throughout the region. Snow ended in the morning in western and central MA, southwest NH, most of CT and RI, and in the early afternoon across eastern MA. ||The Blizzard of 2013 also produced a prolonged period of very strong winds Friday night along the MA and RI coasts.  Gusts exceeded hurricane force (74 mph) at a few locations.  Gale force gusts (to 50 mph) continued on the MA coast through Saturday afternoon.  The strong winds, combined with a wet snow, led to extensive power outages from downed trees and wires in southeast coastal MA and in southern RI.  Elsewhere, farther inland, the snow became drier and did not cling to trees like it did along the south and southeast coast of New England.  Some wind gusts included: 76 mph at Logan Int���l. Airport (Boston, MA), 75 mph at Bedford, MA, 77 mph at Hyannis, MA and 68 mph in Jamestown, RI.  Damaging gusts to 60 mph were recorded as far west as Worcester County, MA. Wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph were common elsewhere in southern New England.| |Minor tidal flooding occurred along the south coasts of Connecticut, Massachusetts, and Rhode Island during times of high tide Friday night and Saturday morning.||The Providence Journal reported that almost 170 people sought treatment for storm-related heart attacks, falls, and other injuries related to the storm at Lifespan network hospitals (which includes 4 major Rhode Island hospitals).  In addition 10 people were hospitalized with carbon monoxide poisoning.  No further information was available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near blizzard conditions were observed on the Automated Surface Observing System at TF Green State Airport (KPVD) for two to three hours prior to the most intense part of the storm. Wind sensors were lost during the height of the storm, making verification difficult. Times are estimated based on surrounding stations and radar data.  The highest wind gust observed was 54 mph at a Weatherflow reporting site at Providence Harbor.

Snow ended in the morning in western and central MA, southwest NH, most of CT and RI, and in the early afternoon across eastern MA. ||The Blizzard of 2013 also produced a prolonged period of very strong winds Friday night along the MA and RI coasts.  Gusts exceeded hurricane force (74 mph) at a few locations.  Gale force gusts (to 50 mph) continued on the MA coast through Saturday afternoon.  The strong winds, combined with a wet snow, led to extensive power outages from downed trees and wires in southeast coastal MA and in southern RI.  Elsewhere, farther inland, the snow became drier and did not cling to trees like it did along the south and southeast coast of New England.  Some wind gusts included: 76 mph at Logan Int���l. Airport (Boston, MA), 75 mph at Bedford, MA, 77 mph at Hyannis, MA and 68 mph in Jamestown, RI.  Damaging gusts to 60 mph were recorded as far west as Worcester County, MA. Wind gusts of 35 to 50 mph were common elsewhere in southern New England.| |Minor tidal flooding occurred along the south coasts of Connecticut, Massachusetts, and Rhode Island during times of high tide Friday night and Saturday morning.||The Providence Journal reported that almost 170 people sought treatment for storm-related heart attacks, falls, and other injuries related to the storm at Lifespan network hospitals (which includes 4 major Rhode Island hospitals).  In addition 10 people were hospitalized with carbon monoxide poisoning.  No further information was available.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Weatherflow reporting site at Quonset Point reported wind gusts up to 63 mph.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong low level jet coupled with strong pressure falls associated with a low pressure region approaching southern New England resulted in strong to damaging winds across southern New England. Damage was largely to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small trees were downed onto Whaley Hollow Road and Phillips Hill Road in Coventry.  A tree was downed onto wires elsewhere in Coventry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong low level jet coupled with strong pressure falls associated with a low pressure region approaching southern New England resulted in strong to damaging winds across southern New England. Damage was largely to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto Howard Hill Road in Foster.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong low pressure region (976mb) moved from Ontario into southern Quebec.  Southern New England was sandwiched in between this and high pressure off the coast to the southeast, creating a very tight pressure gradient over the region.  This resulted in strong gusty winds which caused some damage, especially to areas where the trees were still fully leaved.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on Hope Street in Bristol.  The Automated Surface Observation System at nearby T.F. Green State Airport (KPVD) recorded wind gusts to 52 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto the ramp from Reservoir Avenue to Route 10 Eastbound in Cranston.  The Automated Surface Observation System from the nearby T.F. Green State Airport in Warwick (KPVD) recorded wind gusts of 52 mph.
